summarization.a: The image panel shows the surface morphology of InGaAs after RIE before ALE
summarization.b: The image panel shows the surface morphology of InGaAs after 250 and 600 cycles of ALE at 40m Torr and 250 °C
summarization.c: The image panel shows the surface morphology of InGaAs after 500 cycles of ALE at 140m Torr and 250 °C
summarization.d: The image panel shows the surface morphology of InGaAs after 250 and 600 cycles of ALE at 40m Torr and 300 °C
summarization.e: The line chart illustrates the change in RMS surface roughness of etched InGaAs surface with increasing number of cycles under HF/DMAC at 40 mT and 300°C.
summarization.f: The image panel includes three AFM images showing the surface topography of InGaAs after RIE and after different cycles of thermal ALE at 40 mT and 300°C.

a.question: Does thermal ALE offer advantage in device processing?
a.answer: Thermal ALE can preserve or possibly improve sidewall quality even after hundreds of cycles. The improvement in fin sidewall roughness, in particular, can have a significant impact on FinFET performance as the AFM image shows that the surface topography is preserved after 150 and 200 cycles.

f.question: Can the fin sidewall roughness produced by the RIE process  be reduced after thermal ALE?

e.question: Which ALE process condition provides the smoothest surface?
e.answer:
At lower DMAC pressure and lower temperature, the fin sidewalls become rougher after 250 and 600 cycles (b). In the cases of (c) or (d), with a higher pressure DMAC or higher temperature, the quality of the sidewall and planar surfaces significantly improves. Overall, process (image d) with the higher
temperature of 300 °C for 250 cycles produces the smoothest fin sidewall and planar surfaces.

e.question: Why does the RMS roughness increase after 250 cycles of ALE?
e.answer: The slight increase of Rrms after 250 thermal ALE cycles may occur because InGaAs and InAlAs are ternary materials. When they are etched, the surface can suffer from preferential etching of one metal over the other. XPS analysis  confirmed that the In/Ga ratio decreased from 1.0 to 0.8−0.9 after 600 thermal ALE cycles at 250−300 °C as expected from the preferential etching of InAs compared with GaAs. There was also increased Al content perhaps resulting from partial conversion of InGaAs/InAlAs to AlAs during the DMAC reaction.
